title: Valid Sudoku Backtracking Bug Fix: Unnecessary Backtrack

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: A 9x9 2D list of integers representing the Sudoku grid.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean value indicating whether the Sudoku grid can be solved.
statement.notes: The grid will not be empty and will only contain numbers in the range 0 to 9.

examples.explanation: An example of a valid Sudoku grid

hints.text: Understand the problem and the algorithm
Focus on fixing the unnecessary backtrack

editorial.title: Sudoku Backtracking Bug Fix: Unnecessary Backtrack
editorial.intuition: The Sudoku problem is a classic backtracking problem. To solve it, we need to use a 2D list to represent the Sudoku grid and a helper function to check if it is valid to place a 0 in any position of the grid.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would be to try all possible values and check if it is valid. However, this approach is inefficient as it tries all possible values for each empty cell in the Sudoku grid.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can optimize the backtracking process by only trying values that are valid for each cell and row.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The backtracking process can be proven correct by showing that if the Sudoku grid can be solved, then our function will return True, and if the Sudoku grid cannot be solved, then our function will return False.
editorial.dryRun: First, we initialize an empty Sudoku grid with 0 as the default value for each cell. Then, we call the helper function to start backtracking from an empty Sudoku grid. The helper function will try all possible values for each empty cell in the Sudoku grid and check if it is valid. If the Sudoku grid can be solved, then the helper function will return True and we will print the Sudoku grid.
commonMistakes: Not checking for necessary conditions before backtracking
